volume_title: Volume I — The Foundations of Spark
title: The Shuffle — The Great Enemy

blocks.content: A shuffle is a full redistribution of data across the network between Spark executors. Every row is hashed by its group/join key, and rows with the same hash are sent to the same output partition. This involves: disk writes (shuffle write), network transfer, and disk reads (shuffle read) — the three most expensive operations in distributed computing.

Shuffle = disk write + network transfer + disk read. A job stuck at '99% complete' for hours is almost always waiting for one slow shuffle stage. Look at Spark UI → Stages → shuffle read/write metrics to diagnose.
Sort-merge join (SMJ) is Spark's default join strategy for large tables. It executes in 3 phases:

rows: Each executor hashes rows by join key, writes to local shuffle files partitioned by output partition number
Each executor fetches the shuffle files it owns from all other executors
Each executor sorts its received data by join key, then merges the two sorted streams

blocks.content:
# Force explain to see join strategy
df_orders.join(df_customers, 'customer_id').explain(mode='formatted')

# Output will show:
# == Physical Plan ==
# *(5) SortMergeJoin [customer_id], [customer_id], Inner
# :- *(2) Sort [customer_id ASC]
# :  +- Exchange hashpartitioning(customer_id, 200)
# :     +- *(1) Filter ...
# +- *(4) Sort [customer_id ASC]
#    +- Exchange hashpartitioning(customer_id, 200)
#       +- *(3) Filter ...

# Exchange = shuffle. Two of them for SMJ.
The four main strategies to reduce shuffle cost in production pipelines:

rows: Zero shuffle for small table join
Small table must fit in executor memory
Less data shuffled = less network traffic
Pre-shuffle and sort at write time (Hive tables). Same job avoids shuffle at read time.
Extra write cost; tables must be co-bucketed
Slight AQE overhead; not applicable to all cases

blocks.content:
# Broadcast join (zero shuffle)
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast
result = large_df.join(broadcast(small_df), 'key')

# Bucketed writes — pre-sort and hash for future joins (Hive/Delta tables)
large_df.write \
    .bucketBy(200, 'customer_id') \
    .sortBy('customer_id') \
    .saveAsTable('orders_bucketed')

# Reading two bucketed tables with same bucket column = no shuffle join
spark.read.table('orders_bucketed').join(
    spark.read.table('customers_bucketed'), 'customer_id'
).explain()  # Should show BucketedHashJoin (no Exchange)

Data skew occurs when one join/group key has disproportionately more rows than others. All rows with that key land on one executor partition — causing that one task to process 1000× more data than average. The job can't complete until this straggler finishes.

# Diagnose skew: check key distribution
df.groupBy('customer_id').count() \
  .orderBy(col('count').desc()).show(10)
# If top key has 80% of rows → severe skew

# Fix 1: Use AQE skew join detection (Spark 3.0+)
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled', 'true')
# AQE automatically splits skewed partitions

# Fix 2: Salt the key (manual skew fix)
from pyspark.sql.functions import concat, lit, (col('rand')*10).cast('int').alias('salt')
df_salted = df.withColumn('salt', (rand() * 10).cast('int')) \
              .withColumn('join_key', concat(col('customer_id'), lit('_'), col('salt')))

# Explode the small table to match all 10 salts
from pyspark.sql.functions import explode, array
df_small_salted = df_small.withColumn('salt', explode(array(*[lit(i) for i in range(10)])))\
                          .withColumn('join_key', concat(col('customer_id'), lit('_'), col('salt')))

# Join on salted key — rows distributed across 10 partitions per key
result = df_salted.join(df_small_salted, 'join_key')

At MAANG: always check key skew before joining large tables. A simple groupBy(key).count().orderBy(desc).show(5) takes seconds but can save hours of debugging a stalled job. Set spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled=true as a baseline for all production jobs.

rune_cards.definition: Full network redistribution of data by hash of a key. Involves: shuffle write (disk), network transfer, shuffle read (disk + sort). Most expensive Spark operation.
Default Spark join for large tables. Two shuffles (one per table) + sort + merge. O(N log N) with 2× full data movement over network.
One partition key has disproportionately more rows. Causes one task to run 100-1000× longer than others — the straggler. Fix: AQE skewJoin, salting, or broadcast the skewed dimension.
Append a random suffix (0-N) to a skewed join key to distribute its rows across N partitions. Requires exploding the small table to match all N salt values.
Write strategy: pre-sort and hash data by a join column at write time. When two bucketed tables are joined on the same column with the same bucket count, Spark skips the shuffle entirely.
In a physical plan (explain output), an Exchange hashpartitioning(...) node = a shuffle. Counting Exchange nodes tells you how many shuffles a query has.

quiz.question: A job joining two multi-TB tables is stuck at 99% for 3 hours. One task is processing 10 GB while all others processed ~50 MB. What is the most likely cause and fix?
options: The cluster ran out of disk space — add more disk
Data skew: one join key value has millions of rows (e.g., null or 'unknown'). Fix: enable AQE skewJoin, use salting, or broadcast that key separately
The join strategy is wrong — switch to broadcast join
Too many shuffle partitions — reduce spark.sql.shuffle.partitions to 10
quiz.explanation: 99% stuck with one massive task vs tiny others = classic data skew. A single join key (often null or a default value) has millions of rows that all land on one executor partition (one task). Fix: enable spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled=true for automatic splitting, or manually salt the key. Broadcast join only helps if one table is small — not applicable to multi-TB case.

quiz.question: Your Spark UI shows: Stage 1 (shuffle write): 500 GB written. Stage 2 (shuffle read): 500 GB read, takes 45 minutes. What should you investigate first?
options: The network bandwidth — 500 GB transfer is expected to take 45 min
Whether a broadcast join is possible (small lookup table), or whether there is key skew causing one partition to receive most of the 500 GB
Increase executor memory to 64 GB to cache shuffle files
quiz.explanation: 45 minutes for a shuffle stage needs investigation. First check: is one of the tables small enough for broadcast? (zero shuffle). Second: check the Spark UI task distribution — are a few tasks reading 100× more than others (key skew)? Third: are you shuffling 500 GB when you could filter first to reduce data? More partitions (option D) would help throughput but won't fix a fundamental shuffle-or-skew issue.
